value: 일본 국외 거주 외국인 여권 소지자. 'Temporary Visitor' 체류자격으로 입국한 자만 구매 가능. 일본 국적자 또는 일본 거주 외국인은 구매 불가.

value.비고: 2023년 10월 인상 후 유지. 2025-2026 추가 인상 없음.

value: 한국 국적자는 90일 이내 관광 목적 무비자 입국 가능. 여권 잔여 유효기간 제한 없음 (체류 기간 이상 권장). Visit Japan Web 사전 등록 권장.
Suica/PASMO 등 IC카드로 전철, 버스, 편의점 결제 가능. 물리 카드 재고 부족은 2025.3월 공식 해소. Welcome Suica(관광객용, 28일 유효) 주요 역에서 판매 재개. 모바일 Suica(Apple/Google) 및 Welcome Suica Mobile(iPhone, 180일 유효) 이용 가능.
지정석(Reserved)과 자유석(Non-reserved) 구분. JR Pass 소지 시 지정석 무료 예약 가능 (Nozomi/Mizuho 제외). 자유석은 선착순 탑승.
면세 범위: 주류 3병(760ml 이하), 담배 200개비(거주자)/400개비(비거주자), 향수 2oz, 기타 품목 20만엔 이하. 초과 시 신고 및 관세 부과.

value.비고: 구급차 무료, 치료비는 별도. 119/110은 일본어 우선이나 통역 연결 가능.

value: Visit Japan Web은 웹 기반 서비스(앱 불필요). 여권 정보, 입국심사(하선카드), 세관신고, 면세쇼핑 3개 항목 사전 등록. 등록 후 QR코드 발급 → 공항 도착 시 제시. 2026년 현재 필수는 아니나 강력 권장 (심사 시간 대폭 단축). 스크린샷 불가, 라이브 접속 필요.

신제도_2026_11월.방식: 환급형 (구매 시 세금 포함 → 출국 시 환급)

value: All foreign nationals/people who need to obtain a short-term visa to Japan and currently reside in Australia, Brazil, Cambodia, Canada, Saudi Arabia, South Africa, Taiwan, United Kingdom, and U.S.A. are eligible to apply for a tourist visa online.
Foreigners staying in Japan can use Visit Japan Web for immigration procedures (immigration clearance, customs declaration) and tax-free purchases during their stay.

Foreign visitors in Japan can receive emergency medical assistance including medical referrals, interpretation services, emergency patient transport, hospital arrangements, and assistance with reporting theft or loss of travel documents.
Personal belongings are not subject to customs duties as long as their quantity can be deemed reasonable for normal consumption over a fixed period of time (maximum 90 days for many touristic visas).
The nationwide average monthly rent for a one room apartment (20-40 square meters) is between 50,000 and 70,000 yen, excluding utilities.
Himeji Castle charges 1,000 yen ($7.01) for local residents and 2,500 yen ($17.52) for visitors from outside the city
Icoca card costs ¥2000 including a ¥500 deposit and ¥1500 of credit
One in three respondents in Japan considered the lunch price range of ¥900 to ¥1,199 as expensive; overall, half thought ¥1,200 was expensive.
As of 2026, Suica can be used nationwide in Japan due to the IC card interoperability system, allowing a Suica purchased in Tokyo to be used on subways in Osaka and buses in Fukuoka.
Capsule hotels are located in Matsumoto, Tokyo, and Fukuoka
Many hotels in Japan, especially ryokans, require guests to remove their shoes at the entrance or in their room.
The Japan Rail Pass is a service offered only to foreign nationals traveling to Japan as temporary visitors with a Temporary Visitor visa status.
Tokyo to Kyoto Shinkansen trip takes just over two hours
Most retail shops and department stores in Japan open around 10:00 AM and close around 8:00 PM

Pasmo and Suica are rechargeable prepaid rail cards widely used in the Tokyo area and can be purchased at ticket vending machines in stations throughout Japan including major airports such as Tokyo’s Narita and Haneda.

Many businesses in Japan are cash-only, so travelers should be prepared to carry cash.
In Japan, one in three respondents considered the lunch price range of ¥900 to ¥1,199 expensive; overall, half thought ¥1,200 was expensive.

Pocket Wi-Fi in Japan is a small, portable router that connects to Japan’s mobile data networks and creates a private Wi-Fi hotspot for multiple devices, allowing travelers to share data and avoid compatibility issues with locked phones. It can be reserved and picked up at major airports in Japan.
Tsukiji Outer Market in Tokyo
Pocket WiFi devices and plans can be rented online before arriving in Japan, with flexible pickup and delivery options across Japan, such as from NINJA WiFi.

Wi-FiRental.com monthly plan costs ¥6,600/month, with a ¥1,100 discount available using code 'cheapo'
Visa cards are widely accepted in Japan, allowing purchases, payments, and withdrawals at banks and ATMs.

Travelers should plan ahead and arrange connectivity solutions like pocket WiFi or SIM/eSIM before arriving in Japan to ensure reliable internet access from arrival to departure, avoiding delays at airports and enabling smooth navigation and digital transactions.
The Japanese government has budgeted ¥280 billion (US$2.6 billion) to the end of March 2020, covering both rebates to consumers and subsidies for businesses installing cashless-payment equipment.
Kaiseki is Japan’s most refined dining, almost synonymous with Western haute cuisine, and requires the highest level of etiquette and respect for the food.
The average food cost in Japan is $30–50 per day for most travelers in 2026; budget travelers spend $15–25 per day using convenience stores and chain restaurants.
In Japan, QR code payment platforms such as PayPay allow customers to make purchases via fast money transfers using smartphones.
A deposit is required to buy an IC travel card
Japan Prepaid SIM Card costs $0.96 per day
For travel plans including remote destinations beyond major cities in Japan, choose SIM card providers that utilise the NTT Docomo or Softbank networks for exceptional reliability in the countryside.
You can buy a data-only SIM card at BIC Camera Yurakucho Store near Tokyo Station, located near the Kokusai Forum Entrance.
Visa cards are widely accepted in Japan, allowing purchases, payments, and withdrawals at banks and ATMs.

Although Japan uses many international payment methods such as Apple Pay and Google Pay, domestic payment methods like PayPay are popular and bring QR code payments into the mainstream, allowing fast money transfers.

Tokyo to Nikko by Local Train + Shinkansen takes approximately 6 hours and 30 minutes

Japanese people speak softly or do not talk at all on mass transit, on the street, and in indoor spaces like stores, coffee shops, and restaurants, making public places in Japan noticeably quieter than in the U.S.

Use IC cards like Suica, Pasmo, and ICOCA as rechargeable smart cards for trains, subways, buses, and even vending machines or convenience stores; these cards work across most regions in Japan, including travel between Tokyo and Kyoto.
To use trains in Japan (excluding shinkansen), travelers can buy tickets or use IC cards like Suica or PASMO. Navigating stations and avoiding common mistakes are important for a smooth experience.

In Japan, many businesses operate on a cash-only basis, so travelers should be prepared to carry cash.
Within Tokyo or the largest cities of Japan, assuming you aren't going on day trips, you typically don't spend more than 1000 to 1500 Yen daily on transport.
When staying at a ryokan, guests are expected to remove their shoes at the entrance or in their room and wear slippers provided inside the facility.

Business hotels in Japan are comfortable enough for a good night's sleep, usually located in good locations, have private bathrooms, and offer decent prices, making them a go-to option for travelers staying together.

Capsule hotels in Japan have very limited space for suitcases, often requiring guests to keep their luggage on the bed or blocking the exit if opened; bathrooms are usually all-in-one plastic units with walls, bathtub, floor, and basin made of the same material and color.

It is not customary to tip in Japan, and if you do, restaurant staff will likely return the money to you.

Typical dinner time in Japan generally falls between 6:00 p.m. and 8:00 p.m.

Japanese people speak softly or do not talk at all in public spaces such as mass transit, streets, stores, coffee shops, and restaurants, making places like subway stations quieter than libraries in the U.S.
Convenience stores are open 24 hours a day in Japan

Kaiseki is Japan’s most refined dining, almost synonymous with Western haute cuisine, requiring the highest level of etiquette and respect for the food, typically prepared by experienced professional chefs.
Most Japanese restaurants specialize in serving only one specific type of cuisine, such as fish, chicken, beef, or ramen, and may not serve other types of food.
For popular restaurants in Japan, it’s generally a good idea to make a reservation in advance, unless the restaurant does not accept bookings.
Travelers should review coverage maps during trip planning to ensure reliable mobile internet access across multiple cities and regions in Japan.

Travelers in Japan can use eSIMs by purchasing affordable data plans up to 20 GB, which support a single eSIM for multiple travel destinations without incurring roaming charges. Providers like Saily, Nomad, and Sakura Mobile offer options including free trial eSIMs with 500 MB data for first-time users and discounts for group travelers.

When booking accommodations in Japan, you generally have two options: a ryokan (traditional Japanese inn) or a hotel. Ryokans are typically smaller and more intimate, often including kaiseki ryori (traditional multi-course dinner) in the price for one night, which is a must-have experience.
Tsukiji Outer Market, Tokyo
Many convenience stores such as 7-Eleven, FamilyMart, and Lawson, as well as some chain restaurants and discount stores, operate 24 hours a day, even on national holidays.
It is generally a good idea to make a reservation in advance for popular restaurants in Japan, unless the restaurant does not accept bookings.
Travelers should review coverage maps during trip planning to ensure reliable mobile internet access throughout Japan.

To use Wi-Fi at major Japanese airports such as Narita, Haneda, Kansai, New Chitose, Centrair, and Fukuoka, travelers can connect to free Wi-Fi services provided within the terminals immediately upon arrival. The connection process differs by airport and travelers should check specific airport Wi-Fi instructions.
